summary: The codegen mechanism is correct and the networks.ts refactor is clean. Three minor issues worth addressing before 0.1.0-alpha releases: (1) sideEffects:false conflicts with the module-level console.warn in networks.ts — bundlers will tree-shake that branch away, silencing operator notes; (2) sampleGasCost and gasCostExceedsThreshold from the JSON are silently dropped by the codegen — the gasCostExceedsThreshold flag was previously noted as a follow-up for core.ts startup warning; (3) no .gitignore entry for src/generated/ means the committed generated file and a future clean checkout after JSON change will diverge if prebuild is not run. All three are minor or nit; none block the deploy.

findings.title: Module-level console.warn violates sideEffects:false
findings.description: package.json declares `"sideEffects": false`. This tells bundlers (webpack, rollup, esbuild/tsup consumers) that every import from this package is free of observable side effects and can be tree-shaken if unused. But networks.ts executes a console.warn loop at module load time (lines 30-40), which IS an observable side effect. A consumer that imports only `withPaywall` will have the tree-shaker potentially drop the NETWORKS import entirely — or, depending on the bundler's handling of the flag, the notes will be silently never printed. The behaviour is indeterminate across bundlers. In practice tsup bundles with `splitting: false` so the current `dist/index.js` has the effect inlined and it will fire. But downstream consumers who import from the compiled package into their own bundled app may not.
findings.fix: Either (a) remove `"sideEffects": false` from package.json (safest, since networks.ts has a deliberate side effect), or (b) move the console.warn to a named export function like `logT3Notes()` called explicitly from core.ts startup, which preserves the sideEffects:false claim and is also more testable. Option (b) is preferable: it makes the startup behaviour explicit and easy to suppress or redirect without the env-var hack.
findings.spec_ref: tech-spec D18 / package.json invariants from Task 1

findings.title: sampleGasCost and gasCostExceedsThreshold silently dropped from JSON
findings.description: The source JSON (`contracts/scripts/arc-testnet-usdc-domain.json`) contains `"sampleGasCost": "1260 micro-USDC"` and `"gasCostExceedsThreshold": true`. The codegen's `emitted` object (line 50-56) explicitly lists only `name`, `version`, `decimals`, `supportsEip3009`, `notes` — the other two fields are not passed through. This is intentional for the `ArcTestnetUsdcDomain` type (those fields are not in the interface), but it means the `gasCostExceedsThreshold: true` signal is permanently lost after codegen. Prior audit round (security-auditor-t6-round1.json) flagged this as a Task 8 follow-up: 'surface a warning if gasCostExceedsThreshold is true'. Task 8 is done; it is not clear the warning was added. The notes[] array does carry the gas-fallback note as a string, so operators see it via the boot warn, but the boolean flag is gone. If Task 8 never wired the startup warning, this is now the only place where gasCostExceedsThreshold surfaces — and it has been dropped.
findings.fix: Add `gasCostExceedsThreshold` to the `ArcTestnetUsdcDomain` interface (in both the script and the generated file) and pass it through in `emitted`. Then in networks.ts startup block, check `arcTestnetUsdcDomain.gasCostExceedsThreshold` and emit a more targeted warning distinct from the notes loop. This is the follow-up noted in T6/T8 audits and it is straightforwardly done here.
findings.spec_ref: tech-spec Risks row 'Per-payment settlement creates per-event gas overhead'; security-auditor-t6-round1.json finding on gasCostExceedsThreshold

findings.title: Generated file committed without a src/generated/ gitignore entry
findings.description: The generated file is currently committed to git, which is a reasonable choice for reproducibility (clean checkout always has a working build). However, there is no `.gitignore` entry for `src/generated/` at any level (neither root nor package-local). This creates two risks: (a) a developer who regenerates with a changed JSON will see the generated file as a git-modified file — easy to accidentally stage unrelated changes; (b) if a future CI step runs `git clean -fdx src/generated/` before build, the file disappears and the build will fail if the JSON is not available (e.g. a consumer checkout without the `contracts/` subdir). The committed strategy is correct given the monorepo constraints, but it should be explicit. Add a comment at the top of the generated file (already present: 'Do not edit by hand') AND add a `# src/generated/ is committed intentionally` comment to the root `.gitignore` or the package-level `.gitignore`. This makes the intent clear to future contributors who wonder why a generated file is tracked.
findings.fix: Add `# packages/middleware/src/generated/ is committed intentionally (prebuild codegen, see scripts/generate-arc-testnet-usdc-domain.ts)` to root `.gitignore` as a comment. No behavioral change — purely documents the decision.
findings.spec_ref: task-16 description 'Whether src/generated/ should be gitignored or committed (currently committed — argued for reproducibility)'

findings.title: Type assertion on JSON.parse with no structural validation beyond name+version
findings.description: `JSON.parse(readFileSync(JSON_PATH, 'utf8')) as ArcTestnetUsdcDomain` is a bare cast. The script then validates only `typeof parsed.name !== 'string' || typeof parsed.version !== 'string'`. Fields `decimals` (number), `supportsEip3009` (boolean), and `notes` (array or absent) are not validated — if they are present but wrong type (e.g. `decimals` as string '6'), the generated file would contain a type-mismatched value that passes TypeScript's `as const` but could cause a runtime issue when `arcTestnetUsdcDomain.decimals` is used arithmetically. The codegen is build-time-only so the blast radius is limited, but the `as ArcTestnetUsdcDomain` cast gives false confidence.
findings.fix: Add `typeof parsed.decimals !== 'number'` and `typeof parsed.supportsEip3009 !== 'boolean'` checks to the validation block. Alternatively use a minimal runtime check library or zod schema (but that adds a dep — the manual checks are fine here).
findings.spec_ref: methodology dimension 5 (type safety) / error handling dimension 4

findings.title: Published version is 0.0.1-alpha.1, task spec says 0.1.0-alpha.0
findings.description: Task 16 spec says the target version is `0.1.0-alpha.0` (or `0.1.0-alpha.1` on E409 retry). The deployed version is `0.0.1-alpha.1`. This is a deviation from the task spec. The deployer message confirms `0.0.1-alpha.0` was also published (and is 'broken bundle; pending deprecation'). The version numbering convention (0.0.1 vs 0.1.0) is a pre-release style decision, but the task description explicitly states the version should be `0.1.0-alpha.0`. If this was intentional (e.g. a decision to use 0.0.x for pre-alpha), it should be documented as a deviation per the task's 'Post-completion' checklist.
findings.fix: Document in decisions.md (task post-completion checklist item) why the version was `0.0.1-alpha.*` rather than `0.1.0-alpha.*`. No code change needed if the decision was intentional.

positive_observations: Codegen mechanism is correct and idempotent — the prev !== body guard prevents spurious file writes, making CI diffs clean.
Error handling in generate-arc-testnet-usdc-domain.ts is solid: file-missing and field-missing cases both throw with actionable messages and process.exit(1), not swallowed.
The refactor in networks.ts from runtime readFileSync to static import is the right architectural fix — eliminates the monorepo path dependency at runtime for npm consumers.
The prebuild hook placement in package.json is correct and will run before both `npm run build` and `prepublishOnly`, ensuring the generated file is always fresh in CI.
networks.ts dual-key aliasing (both 'arc-testnet' and 'eip155:5042002' pointing at the same object reference) is preserved correctly.
The ESLint ignore for packages/middleware/scripts/ uses the same pattern and rationale as the existing __tests__/ ignore — consistent.
Test update from 'enabled false until Task 11' to 'enabled after Task 16 live deploy' correctly reflects the new state.
Bundle size 9.1 KB gzipped is well within the 30 KB limit.
notes_for_next_round: CR-T16-1 (sideEffects vs console.warn) is the most impactful of the minor issues and worth a follow-up fix in the next alpha. CR-T16-2 (gasCostExceedsThreshold dropped) is the second priority. Both can be addressed in 0.0.1-alpha.2 or 0.1.0-alpha.0 without blocking the current deploy.
